audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel / Shemuel Bet chapter 1, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for the messenger's conflicting Saul-death account, Amalekite identity, crown and bracelet, mourning, summary execution, the Bow lament, Jonathan-David love language, women mourners, Gilboa curse, and weapon-fall refrain.

verses.restored: After death of Saul, David returned from striking Amalek and stayed two days in Ziklag.
On third day, look, a man came from camp, from with Saul, with garments torn and earth upon his head. When he came to David, he fell to ground and bowed.
David said to him, From where have you come? He said to him, From camp of Israel I escaped.
David said to him, What was matter? Please tell me. He said, People fled from battle, and also many of people fell and died; Saul and Jonathan his son also died.
David said to young man who told him, How do you know that Saul and Jonathan his son died?
Young man who told him said, By chance I was on mount Gilboa, and look, Saul was leaning upon his spear, and look, chariots and horsemen pressed close to him.
He turned behind him, saw me, and called to me. I said, Look, I am here.
He said to me, Who are you? I said to him, I am an Amalekite.
He said to me, Please stand over me and put me to death, because agony has seized me, though all my life is still in me.
I stood over him and put him to death, because I knew he could not live after his fall. I took crown that was upon his head and bracelet that was upon his arm, and I brought them here to my lord.
David took hold of his garments and tore them, and so did all men who were with him.
They mourned, wept, and fasted until evening for Saul, for Jonathan his son, for people of YHWH, and for house of Israel, because they had fallen by sword.
David said to young man who told him, From where are you? He said, I am son of a resident alien, an Amalekite.
David said to him, How were you not afraid to stretch out your hand to destroy anointed of YHWH?
David called one of young men and said, Approach; strike him. He struck him, and he died.
David said to him, Your blood is upon your head, because your mouth testified against you, saying, I put anointed of YHWH to death.
David lamented with this lament over Saul and over Jonathan his son.
He said to teach children of Judah the Bow. Look, it is written in Book of Jashar/Upright.
Beauty/glory of Israel is slain upon your high places. How have mighty fallen!
Do not tell it in Gath; do not announce it in streets of Ashkelon, lest daughters of Philistines rejoice, lest daughters of uncircumcised exult.
Mountains of Gilboa, let there be no dew and no rain upon you, nor fields of offerings, because there shield of mighty was defiled, shield of Saul, no longer anointed with oil.
From blood of slain, from fat of mighty, bow of Jonathan did not turn back, and sword of Saul did not return empty.
Saul and Jonathan, beloved and pleasant in their lives, and in their death they were not divided; swifter than eagles, stronger than lions.
Daughters of Israel, weep over Saul, who clothed you in scarlet with delights, who placed ornaments of gold upon your clothing.
How have mighty fallen in midst of battle! Jonathan is slain upon your high places.
I am distressed for you, my brother Jonathan. Very pleasant you were to me. Wonderful was your love to me, surpassing love of women.
How have mighty fallen, and weapons of war perished!

translation_notes: The messenger's claim that he killed Saul conflicts with 1 Samuel 31, where Saul falls on his own sword. The narrative may portray a true report, a mercy-killing claim, opportunistic fabrication, or a separate tradition; the tension must remain visible.
The messenger is both Amalekite and son of a resident alien. His ethnicity does not explain guilt and must not support inherited enmity, xenophobia, or modern Amalek labeling.
David orders immediate execution based on the man's own testimony. The text provides no trial, witnesses, or opportunity for defense; this is summary royal violence, not a due-process model.
Saul's alleged request to be killed occurs under catastrophic battlefield injury. It may not become instruction for assisted death, suicide, or killing a distressed person.
David's lament honors Saul without erasing Saul's violence against David, Michal, Jonathan, priests, and civilians. Grief does not require historical absolution.
Jonathan's love is described as wonderful and surpassing the love of women. Queer readings may not be erased, while no single modern identity label should be forced upon the ancient relationship.
The lament's contemptuous circumcision language and silencing of Philistine women belong to ancient wartime rhetoric and may not authorize genital shame, misogyny, or ethnic hatred.
The final refrain calls warriors and weapons fallen; it is lament, not war recruitment.
safety_note: Maximum safeguards: Do not glorify the messenger's execution, use 'anointed' language to create leader immunity, or prescribe suicide/assisted death from the conflicting report. Preserve Jonathan and David's covenantal intimacy without queer erasure. Let lament hold love, anger, ambiguity, and accountability together.
